lists.title: Russian A1 Grammar Path
lists.description: A1 grammar in TORFL-elementary order: gender and 'это' sentences first, then present-tense verbs, the first cases (prepositional for place, accusative for objects), possessives, past tense, and the genitive of absence.

points.title: Gender of nouns (он, она, оно)
points.function: Refer back to people and things
points.explanation: Every noun is masculine, feminine, or neuter — usually visible in the ending: consonant = masculine (дом), -а/-я = feminine (книга), -о/-е = neuter (окно). The pronoun that replaces a noun matches its gender: Где дом? — Он там. Где книга? — Она на столе.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)

drills.translation: Where is the book? — It is on the table.
Where is the house? — It is there.

points.title: Это — this is / it is
points.explanation: 'Это' plus a noun makes a complete sentence — Russian drops 'to be' in the present: Это дом (This is a house), Это мой друг (This is my friend). Negate with не: Это не школа.
points.culture_note: No articles and no present-tense 'to be': 'Я студент' is literally 'I student' — and completely correct.

points.title: Present tense (читать, говорить)
points.explanation: First conjugation (читать): читаю, читаешь, читает, читаем, читаете, читают. Second conjugation (говорить): говорю, говоришь, говорит, говорим, говорите, говорят. The ending alone tells you the person, so pronouns are often kept for clarity but the verb does the work.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)

points.title: Prepositional case (location: в / на)
points.explanation: The prepositional case marks location after в (in) and на (on). It only ever appears after a preposition, which is why it is taught first among the cases: в Москве (in Moscow), на столе (on the table). Singular endings are usually -е.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)

drills.translation: The book is on the table.

points.title: Accusative case (direct object)
points.function: Say what the action is done to
points.explanation: The direct object takes the accusative. Feminine -а becomes -у (книга → книгу, газета → газету); masculine inanimate nouns don't change (читаю журнал). Animate masculine nouns borrow the genitive form — that contrast comes at A2.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)

points.title: Possessive pronouns (мой, моя, моё)
points.explanation: Possessives agree with the gender of the THING owned, not the owner: мой дом (m), моя книга (f), моё окно (n), мои книги (pl). The same pattern covers твой (your), наш (our), ваш (your, formal/plural).

points.title: Past tense (-л)
points.explanation: Drop -ть and add -л, then agree with the SUBJECT's gender: он читал, она читала, оно читало, они читали. Even 'to be' reappears in the past: он был, она была, они были.
points.culture_note: The past tense agrees in gender, not person — a historical participle. A woman says 'я читала', a man 'я читал'.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)

points.title: Genitive of absence (у меня нет …)
points.function: Say what you have and don't have
points.explanation: Possession is 'у + person + есть': У меня есть брат (I have a brother). Absence is 'нет + genitive': У меня нет брата (I don't have a brother — брат takes genitive -а). This у-construction is the everyday way to say 'have'; the verb иметь is bookish.

references.title: Wikipedia: Test of Russian as a Foreign Language (TORFL)
